so that was kind of how I got into this, just the general area of sales, you know, leading that company.
And then I led sales for a few, for two startups after that.
And then we started Noble with my co-founder, Josh, who I met him back in 2014, because he started his first company at the same time that we were in the same incubator together.
And the original idea behind Noble was around word of mouth recommendations.
So it was, you know, there's so much noise in the space, there's a billion different solutions and vendors, like how do you know what actually works?
And the initial idea was like, you know, what if you could just see what your friends used a product, and you could just message them and ask them about their experience.
And it was working pretty well.
We just ran into a lot of legal and privacy issues, especially as we went to Noble Market.
And then at the same time, once these LLMs came out, it became very clear that before you turn to your friends, you're going to be asking Google and JGBT for feedback.
And advice and recommendations.
And.
And as we talked to marketers about kind of how they're viewing the LLMs, universally, it was like a top two priority for them.
And like for a lot of people, whether it was the marketer or the head of SEO, it the first time that the CEO or the board really cared about what they were doing.
And so for a lot of these marketing teams, we would talk to them and they were like, we just need to figure out how to show up in these LLMs.
And it was for those category level searches.
So it wasn't just like a, how does growthx works?
It is like, you know, what are the best B2B marketing agencies or like, who should I be talking to?
So those types of searches were particularly interesting because one, they're more mid to kind of bottom of funnel.
So the discovery aspect is very valuable for brands.
And two, the sources that these LLMs are citing are not typically the brand's websites.
It's other either publishers or third party sites.
That it's just more difficult for these brands to get mentioned in those sources.
So.
That's kind of how we came into this problem.
So we very much approach it from not your traditional SEO perspective, it's how are people buying, and what is the future of commerce, and what is the direction that things are going.

And, you know, we're not obviously there today, but we're almost seeing the internet kind of condensed down into these LLMs, where I think in the foreseeable future, we're going to have this experience where you go to the LLM, you describe your problem, you describe what you need.
And then you're seeing this with checkout through ChatGPT, but you can actually probably like book a demo or checkout directly from the LLMs in the future.
I think the interface is going to improve, I don't think it's just going to be a pure chat in the future as well to support that.
But this all kind of supports this idea that if you're a brand, the amount that you've invested in your website is going to be equally important that you invest that money to show up in the LLMs.
And a lot of it is like, what are other people and sources saying about you and your brand?
And how accurately is that being reflected across the web?
So that's kind of how we come across this.

Yeah, so I guess there's a few things.
One is like, we're focused exclusively on brand mentions and not backlinks.
Like from an LLM perspective, the links, they matter, but not nearly as much as the copy in the brand mention, because we're adding like paragraphs or multiple sentences into these articles.
And most of the time they come with a link, but when it comes to LLMs and what they value, it's the copy that's added into these articles versus the backlink.
So that's kind of the first thing.
The second thing is we only go after sources that are already being cited for these prompts.
So we're not going after random articles to get them included.
We'll essentially run the prompts for the brand that they care about.
We'll see what the sources are and then only go after the sources.
So from a quality perspective, we know that if the LLM.

LLMs are citing them, then getting mentioned in those sources means that, you know, it's more likely that because those pages, they get updated, those updates will get pulled into the LLM, it'll be more likely to show up in the answer.
So every mention is high quality because we're only getting those brands mentioned in existing citations today.
So that's kind of how we deal with those two things.
And also it's just like the reason that this is so different from backlinks is one is the copy, two is like you look in citations, and then three, the speed in which these LLMs are updated.
So with backlinks and traditional blue link searches, you know, it may take months or quarters for the algorithm to get updated.
With LLMs, it could be days or weeks.
So it's much faster, it actually impacts what the buyer or the prospect is reading versus, you know, the backlink.
You you still have to rely on people clicking the link and then, you know, increasing the ranking of that.
So just kind of how we look at them.
Market in terms of mentions versus backlinks.
And then in terms of like the outreach and how we do it, it's we reach out to the publisher or the author on behalf of the brand.
So we'll say like we represent a brand like Ramp that we think would be a good fit for this article.
And then we'll explain why in the email that we're sending them.
When we send just cool emails to these authors, we get like an 8 to 10% reply rate.
And then of those, about half of them will convert to a mention.
So for cold email, it's really not bad at all as a starting point.
And then once they once like that, the author or that the publishers in our network, then the mention just shows up in the dashboard and then they can accept it.
So it's no longer like a cold email.
They're just like logged into our dashboard and then they see the offer that we're presenting for them.
If it's like a third party publisher in like a kind of a magazine or journal, they want to be mostly paid.
website.
you.
If it's another brand that has a blog post or something, they don't care about the money, they just want the reciprocal mention.
So what's kind of in it for them is it's okay, I'll mention Ramp in this article, but Ramp will mention me on their site or somebody else will, and that's kind of what they care about.
But the more kind of brands that we sign up, the more kind of exchanges we can do across our network.
And so that's why this kind of becomes a marketplace ultimately, is because we can connect brands with publishers through Noble.

So the way we typically work with agencies, so, like, if you know, like, Ethan Smith at Graphite, is essentially they know that they need to get mentioned in these sources, especially for these, like, mid to bottom of funnel searches.
And I, going to your point earlier, it's, like, does it help to create content on your website?
Like, yes, it's better than nothing, but if that content is not being cited, then it's probably not being picked up.
And so it's, like, you can create a ton of content on your site, but it may not actually help move the needle.
When it comes to these types of searches that are more at the category level, I think creating content on your website is great for bottom of funnel searches.
Like, the LLMs are going to go directly to your website if it's, like, branded search.
But when it comes to these mid-funnel searches, it's really about how everybody else is talking about you.
And so that's kind of where this really shines and where it's meant to be placed.
With other SEO agencies, they do one of two things.

One is if they just need to increase overall visibility, they will just pass on the cost of Noble to their client, and they'll just bundle it in to make their solution stickier and to improve their overall results.
The second thing that they'll do is they'll try to upsell it to their clients, where they'll essentially say, like, hey, Noble's cost is $200 per mention.
So they'll go to a client and say, hey, we'll do this for $300 or $400 per target mention per month.
And so they'll kind of bake in margin that way.

If you look at this from, like, a margin perspective, like, I wouldn't look at it as, like, oh, you have 50% gross margins if you charge, you know, $400 and you're paying Noble to $200 per target mention.
I think it's more of just, like, you're collecting margin that you...
You wouldn't have otherwise because you don't really have like a cost per sale.
Like the other way we do this is just like a referral fee.
So it's like if you introduce us to your existing clients and they want this for mentions and just placement and citations, we could also do some sort of like referral fee.
That way so you get a bonus and there's no like cost.
So it's just pure margin at that point.
